policy_statement: Permeabilise or disconnect from sewers at least 250,000 m² of impermeable surfaces in the Molenbeek catchment priority intervention zone.
mechanism_description: The City Council is committed to making hard surfaces permeable or disconnecting them from sewers to reduce stormwater runoff entering the sewer system and improve drainage resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavy rainfall) → ↓ stormwater runoff/peak flow to sewers → ↓ sewer surcharge/CSO + ↓ surface flooding → ↓ exposure and damages
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Retrofit impervious areas (e.g., pavements, parking lots, roofs) with permeable materials and/or disconnect downspouts/drains from combined sewers to route water to infiltration, storage, or green/blue infrastructure. (2) Process: Increased infiltration, detention, and delayed conveyance reduce runoff volume and peak discharge into sewer networks, lowering hydraulic overload and backflow during storm events. (3) Outcome: Reduced pluvial flooding and combined sewer overflow frequency/severity, improving drainage resilience under more intense rainfall.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood and combined-sewer-overflow impacts during heavier rainfall by lowering stormwater runoff volumes and peak flows entering the sewer system.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: managing heavy-rainfall runoff to prevent sewer overload and flooding (hazard → reduced exposure/impact).  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: the primary intent and largest effect is resilience to precipitation extremes (flood/CSO reduction), not direct GHG reduction (Mitigation) or primarily reducing resource inputs (Resource Efficiency).  
3) Edge cases applied: if permeabilization is implemented mainly as green infrastructure that also sequesters carbon or cools urban areas, those are co-benefits; the defining mechanism here is runoff reduction via permeability/disconnection, which is adaptation-focused.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: any policy whose core mechanism is making surfaces permeable and/or disconnecting from sewers to reduce runoff into the sewer system will chiefly reduce flood/overflow risk under extreme rainfall.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., flood risk reduction, CSO reduction, heat co-benefits) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the intervention is framed/implemented primarily for water quality regulation or groundwater recharge (rather than flood/CSO resilience), classification could shift toward Resource Efficiency or multi-benefit framing; however, under this canonical mechanism (stormwater runoff reduction to improve drainage resilience), Adaptation remains dominant.
key_indicators: "City Council is committed"; "permeabilising or disconnecting from the sewers"; "at least 250,000m² of impermeable surfaces"; "priority intervention zone"; "Molenbeek catchment area"
co_benefits: Urban heat mitigation (more permeable/green surfaces), water quality improvement (reduced CSO), biodiversity/amenity improvements.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be read as a target/pledge because it specifies a numeric area, but the action described is an on-the-ground retrofit measure, so it is treated primarily as infrastructure-investment rather than target-setting.
